title: The Young That Died In Beauty
content:
If souls should only sheen so bright
In heaven as in eï¿½thly light,
Anï¿½ nothen better wer the cease,
How comely still, in sheape anï¿½ feace,
Would many reach thik happy pleace, 
The hopevul souls that in their prime
Haï¿½ seemï¿½d aï¿½took avore their time, 
The young that died in beauty.

But when wooneï¿½s limï¿½s haï¿½ lost their strangth
Aï¿½tweilen drough a lifetimeï¿½s langth,
Anï¿½ over cheaks a-growen wold
The slowly-weasten years haï¿½ rollï¿½d
The deepï¿½nen wrinkleï¿½s hollow vwold;
When life is ripe, then death do call
Vor less ov thought, than when do vall
On young voï¿½ks in their beauty.

But pinen souls, wiï¿½ heads a-hung
In heavy sorrow vor the young,
The sister ov the brother dead,
The father wiï¿½ a child aï¿½vled,
The husband when his bride haï¿½ laid
Her head at rest, noo mwore to turn,
Have all a-vound the time to murn
Vor youth that died in beauty.

Anï¿½ yeet the church, where prayer do rise
Vrom thoughtvul souls, wiï¿½ downcast eyes,
Anï¿½ village greens, aï¿½beat half beare
By dancers that do meet, anï¿½ wear
Such merry looks at feast anï¿½ feair,
Do gather under leatest skies,
Their bloomen cheaks anï¿½ sparklen eyes,
Though young haï¿½ died in beauty.

But still the dead shall mwore than keep
The beauty ov their early sleep;
Where comely looks shall never wear
Uncomely, under tweil an' ceare.
The feair at death be always feair,
Still feair to liversï¿½ thought anï¿½ love,
Anï¿½ feairer still to God above,
Than when they died in beauty.
meaning:
About: William Barnes (22nd February 1801 - 7th October 1886) was an English writer, poet, minister, and philologist. 
He wrote over 800 poems, some in Dorset dialect and much other work including a comprehensive English grammar quoting from more than 70 different languages.
